metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `verify-authorship`/`verify-identity` alternative to a single command and add an anchored output assertion (e.g., `rebar verify-authorship "$TICKET_ID" | jq -e '.status == "verified" and .identity_id == "594c-9dcf-5ad6-4e6d"'`) so the proving command cannot false-positive.
findings.finding: AC2's verification command is stated as `rebar verify-authorship` OR `rebar verify-identity` with no anchor for the expected output string, so the proving command has an unresolved 'or' and no assertion on what 'verified' looks like in structured output.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'confirm `rebar verify-authorship` (or `rebar verify-identity`) reports it `verified`'
findings.suggested_fix: Use: `rebar verify-authorship "$TICKET_ID" | jq -e '.status == "verified" and .identity_id == "594c-9dcf-5ad6-4e6d"'`; command exits non-zero if either condition fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state how the committer email is changed to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` — either (a) update the existing 'Configure git identity' step's YAML to hard-code or default to the bot email, or (b) set the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` repo variable — so the email used by `_git_email()` / `_match_by_email()` actually resolves to the Rebar Bot identity ticket.
findings.finding: The plan says 'set the committer email to joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com' but the existing 'Configure git identity' step in the workflow reads `${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com' }}` — if neither the YAML step nor the repo variable is updated, the default email 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com' is set, _match_by_email() finds no identity ticket with that email, author_id is absent from the event envelope, _apply_authorship() skips signing (fast-path: no author_id), and all events are written unsigned, silently breaking AC#2.
findings.location: Plan body ('Set the canary's committer email…') and AC#1; existing reconcile-bridge-canary.yml lines 97-104
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan body, replace 'Set the canary's committer email to joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com' with: 'Update the BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL repo variable to joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com (or hard-code the fallback in the Configure git identity step) so git config user.email resolves to the Rebar Bot identity for email-fallback attribution.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC2's verification protocol to match the actual `rebar verify-identity` output schema: specify the exact invocation (e.g., `rebar verify-identity --all --format json`), and clarify that a `verified` outcome is confirmed by the created ticket's events being ABSENT from the JSON report (since verified events are omitted) or by exit-0 with the 'OK — every enforced in-scope event is verified' message.
findings.finding: AC2's verification success criterion states `rebar verify-authorship` should report the event 'verified' and 'attributed to identity 594c-9dcf-5ad6-4e6d', but the tool's actual output schema (verify_identity_report.schema.json) emits only verdict/display classifications per NON-verified event — verified events are explicitly OMITTED from the JSON report. The string 'attributed to identity <id>' matches no field in the schema, leaving the executor without a concrete decision rule for what output constitutes a pass.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2 — 'confirm rebar verify-authorship (or rebar verify-identity) reports it verified and attributed to identity 594c-9dcf-5ad6-4e6d'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC2 verification clause with: 'Run `rebar verify-identity --all` (exit 0) and confirm the stdout summary line reads "verify-identity: OK — every enforced in-scope event is verified"; optionally run with `--format json` and confirm the created ticket_id does not appear in the report (verified events are omitted from the JSON output).'

findings.finding: AC2 references `verify-identity` as a proving command but does not specify the full invocation, the artifact to pass to it (e.g. ticket/comment ID from a triggered canary run), or the expected output, so it cannot be evaluated without guessing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A canary-created bug/comment/transition is signed as Rebar Bot (verifiable via `verify-identity`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC2 to specify the exact command and flags needed to confirm attribution (e.g., `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq '.[] | select(.ticket_id == "<created-id>")'` and/or inspecting the raw event's `author_sig.payload` for `principal: 594c-9dcf-5ad6-4e6d`), so the executor can unambiguously confirm the event is signed as Rebar Bot.
findings.finding: AC2 instructs the executor to confirm `rebar verify-authorship` 'reports it `verified` and attributed to identity `594c-9dcf-5ad6-4e6d`', but the `verify-authorship` / `verify-identity` command does not emit per-ticket attribution to a named identity id in its default output — it only reports signed/unsigned/unverified status. Confirming attribution to a specific identity id requires `--format json` (to inspect `author_id` fields on each event) and a targeted query against the created ticket id. Without specifying these flags, the AC verification step is underspecified and may lead an executor to conclude 'verified' when the bot identity is not actually the signing principal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'confirm `rebar verify-authorship` (or `rebar verify-identity`) reports it `verified` and attributed to identity `594c-9dcf-5ad6-4e6d`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's parenthetical '(from the current bridge default)' — the current canary default is `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com`, not `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`; describe this as an explicit override to the bot email, not a preservation of an existing default.
findings.finding: The plan's parenthetical '(from the current bridge default)' when setting the committer email to joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com is a false codebase assertion. The actual current default in reconcile-bridge-canary.yml is rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com (via BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL variable fallback), not joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com. This means the plan mis-describes the change being made — it is not preserving a default but overriding to a new value — which could mislead a reviewer or implementer into thinking the email is already set correctly and skipping the change.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Set the canary's committer email to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` (from the current bridge default)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change to: 'Set the canary's committer email to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` (overriding the current workflow default of `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com`) so git-email resolution maps to the Rebar Bot identity.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly how the committer email is set to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` in the workflow (e.g., add a dedicated `git config --global user.email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` step or set `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL: joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` in the job-level `env:`) so that `resolve_current_identity()` can match the Rebar Bot identity ticket regardless of the repo variable's current value.
findings.finding: The plan's identity-resolution chain depends on `git config user.email` matching the Rebar Bot identity ticket's email (`joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`), but the existing 'Configure git identity' step in `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` reads `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` (defaulting to `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com`). If `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` is set at the repo/org level to the non-bot address, the plan's instruction to 'set the committer email to joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com' is ambiguous — it does not specify whether to change the variable, override the step inline, or add a separate `git config` call. A wrong email means `resolve_current_identity()` returns `None`, `author_id` is absent from the event envelope, and signing is silently skipped despite the key being injected correctly.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Set the canary's committer email to joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com'; reconcile-bridge-canary.yml — 'Configure git identity' step (lines 97–104)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated step (or extend the existing 'Configure git identity' step) with `git config --global user.email 'joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com'` hardcoded — not via the `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` variable, which may be set to a different value at the org level.

findings.finding: The plan describes the committer email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` as 'from the current bridge default', but the actual current default in `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` is `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` (via `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com'`). The email is not the current default — it is a new value being introduced. This false premise could cause an implementer to underestimate scope (thinking no email change is needed) or omit the email flip step entirely.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Set the committer email to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` (from the current bridge default)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'from the current bridge default' with 'replacing the current default `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com`' to make clear this is a change, not a pre-existing state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete ticket-id retrieval command or log-grep pattern to AC2 (e.g., `rebar create bug ... | jq -r '.id'` captured to `$TICKET_ID`) so the verify step is deterministic.
findings.finding: AC2 assumes the created ticket id is obtainable from the run log ('capture the created ticket id from the run log') but the plan does not specify what log line or command produces this value, leaving the executor without a concrete retrieval step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'capture the created ticket id from the run log'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC2: 'capture the created ticket id via `TICKET_ID=$(rebar create bug ... | jq -r ".id")` (or grep `"created ticket" <run-log>` for the id pattern), then run `rebar verify-authorship "$TICKET_ID"`.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pick exactly one configuration mechanism in the plan body and AC1 — given the workflow context, commit to `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` (env var, already shown in the example code) and drop the `identity.signing_key` alternative, or explicitly state which is preferred and when the other would be used.
findings.finding: The plan body offers two co-equal config mechanisms — `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` env var OR `identity.signing_key` config key — connected with 'or' and no decision rule. The codebase confirms these are not equivalent: the env var overrides the config key (_config_schema.py comment: 'Env override: REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY'). An executor implementing this plan does not know which to use, creating an ambiguous processing protocol.
findings.location: Plan body — 'export REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY (or set identity.signing_key)' and AC1 — 'sets REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY/identity.signing_key to that path'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan body and AC1, use only `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` (the env var path already illustrated in the concrete step example), and remove the `(or set identity.signing_key)` parenthetical, since the env var is the right choice for ephemeral CI key injection.

findings.finding: The plan says 'set `identity.signing_key` to [the injected file]' but `identity.signing_key` in `rebar.toml` is a committed static value — a per-run temp-file path cannot be written there. The only verified runtime injection mechanism is the env var `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` (documented in `_config_schema.py` line 314, used in tests). The plan does not name this mechanism, leaving the core capability assertion — that signing will activate automatically with no code change — unresolved at the implementation level.
findings.location: Plan body — 'set `identity.signing_key` to it'
findings.suggested_fix: In the workflow step, export `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` pointing to the injected tmpfile (e.g., `echo "${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}" > /tmp/rebar_bot_key && chmod 600 /tmp/rebar_bot_key` then `env: REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY: /tmp/rebar_bot_key` on the rebar CLI steps), rather than editing `rebar.toml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State which configuration mechanism takes precedence (`REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` env var vs `identity.signing_key` config key) so the executor does not have to choose.
findings.finding: The plan gives two configuration mechanisms — `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` env var OR `identity.signing_key` config key — without stating which one the executor should use, leaving a real implementation decision to the executor.
findings.location: Plan body, secure key injection bullet: 'export `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` (or set `identity.signing_key`) to that path'
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one and state it explicitly, e.g.: 'export `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` to the key-file path (preferred; no config-file edit needed).' Remove the alternative from AC1 as well.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the mechanism by which `identity.signing_key` is set to the injected file path. The config key can be set via `rebar.toml` (static) or the env override `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` (documented in `_config_schema.py` line 314). At CI runtime, only the env var path is viable (mutating `rebar.toml` would create a dirty commit), but the plan is silent on this — leaving the implementer to discover the env var or attempt an unsupported runtime toml edit.
findings.location: Plan body: 'set identity.signing_key to it'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a workflow step: `echo "${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}" > /tmp/rebar-bot-signing-key && chmod 0600 /tmp/rebar-bot-signing-key` and set `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY: /tmp/rebar-bot-signing-key` in the job-level `env:` block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consolidate the 'key is never logged or committed' requirement into one criterion (either AC#1 or AC#3) to avoid near-duplicate acceptance gates.
findings.finding: AC#1 and AC#3 both state 'the key is never logged or committed,' creating a near-duplicate sub-requirement across two separate criteria.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC#1 and AC#3
findings.suggested_fix: Remove 'the key is never logged or committed' from AC#3, keeping AC#3 focused solely on the no-code-change constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit non-empty guard after writing the key file (e.g., `test -s "$RUNNER_TEMP/rebar-bot-signing-key" || { echo 'ERROR: REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY secret is empty or unset'; exit 1; }`) so a missing/empty secret fails fast with a clear diagnostic message.
findings.finding: The plan writes the signing key from `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` directly to a file without first asserting the secret is non-empty. If the secret is unset or misconfigured, `printf '%s' ''` writes a zero-byte file, `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` points to it, and `rebar` may either silently produce unsigned events or fail with a cryptic key-format error rather than a clear 'secret not configured' failure — making misconfiguration hard to diagnose.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Secure key injection (concrete)' step; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix:
After the `printf` write, add:
```sh
test -s "$RUNNER_TEMP/rebar-bot-signing-key" || \
  { echo '::error::REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY secret is empty or not configured'; exit 1; }
```
This uses GHA's `::error::` annotation for visibility in the Actions UI and exits before `rebar` is invoked with a broken key path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a guard in the key-injection step that fails fast when `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` is empty (e.g. `[[ -n "$REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY" ]] || { echo '::error::REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY secret is unset'; exit 1; }`) so a misconfigured or missing secret surfaces as a red workflow step rather than a silent unsigned-write.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no guard for an empty or unset `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY`: if the secret is missing or empty, `printf '%s' "" > $RUNNER_TEMP/rebar-bot-signing-key` writes a zero-byte file, `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` points to it, `sign_event_authorship` fails to parse the key, the exception is caught and logged as a warning, and events are written unsigned — the job does not fail and the silent unsigned writes violate AC#2 and AC#3.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Secure key injection (concrete)' / `printf '%s' "${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}" > ...`
findings.suggested_fix: After `echo '::add-mask::...'` and before writing the file, add: `[[ -n "${REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY}" ]] || { echo '::error::REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY is empty or unset — cannot sign canary events'; exit 1; }`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a guard step (or inline assertion) after writing the key file that fails the job immediately if `$RUNNER_TEMP/rebar-bot-signing-key` is absent or zero-bytes (e.g., `[ -s "$RUNNER_TEMP/rebar-bot-signing-key" ] || { echo '::error::REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY secret is empty or unset'; exit 1; }`) so a missing secret produces a hard failure rather than silent unsigned writes.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a guard that fails fast when `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` is absent or empty. If the secret is unset, `printf '%s' "${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}"` writes a zero-byte file; `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` points to it; and `_seam._apply_authorship` (which is best-effort) silently falls through to writing unsigned events — so canary tickets are created without authorship signatures and AC#2 (`rebar verify-authorship` reports `verified`) silently fails while the workflow exits 0.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Secure key injection (concrete)' section; AC#2 (verify-authorship reports `verified`); AC#3 ('the key is never logged or committed')
findings.suggested_fix: After the `printf` line, add: `[ -s "$RUNNER_TEMP/rebar-bot-signing-key" ] || { echo '::error::REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY is empty or unset — aborting to prevent unsigned ticket writes'; exit 1; }`. This converts a silent secret-lifecycle failure into an immediately visible workflow error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete verification step to AC3 for the 'never logged' claim, e.g.: 'in the workflow_dispatch run log, search for the literal secret value (first 8 chars) and confirm it is absent or replaced by `***`.'
findings.finding: AC3's claim 'the key is never logged' has no concrete proving command — the plan describes adding `::add-mask::` but no step verifies the mask actually suppressed the secret in the GHA run log.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'the key is never logged or committed'
findings.suggested_fix: After the run, download the GHA log artifact and run: `grep -c "$(echo $REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY | cut -c1-8)" run.log` expecting 0 matches (or confirm GHA UI shows `***` wherever the value would appear).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the committer email is an existing value or a new change, and add an explicit rollback path (e.g., a feature flag, a step to disable signing, or a revert instruction) for the case where key injection or identity resolution fails.
findings.finding: The plan adds signing behavior to previously unsigned canary events and ambiguously describes the committer-email change ('from the current bridge default'), but provides no rollback path if the key injection or identity resolution fails mid-run.
findings.location: Plan body — second bullet; Acceptance Criteria — AC#1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lifecycle note for `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY`: specify who provisions it (and when, relative to merging this change), the rotation owner/cadence, and add a workflow guard (e.g. `if: env.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY != ''` or an explicit `test -s` check) that fails the job loudly rather than proceeding with an empty key.
findings.finding: The plan references `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` without specifying (a) who is responsible for creating/provisioning this secret in the GitHub repository, (b) what the rotation cadence or ownership is, or (c) what the workflow does when the secret is absent or expired — the job-step will silently write an empty file and `rebar` will sign with a blank key, producing quietly invalid signatures.
findings.location: Secure key injection section / AC item 1 (`REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY`)
findings.suggested_fix:
Before the mask/write steps, add:
```yaml
- name: Validate signing key
  run: |
    if [ -z "$REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY" ]; then
      echo "::error::REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY secret is not set"
      exit 1
    fi
  env:
    REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY: ${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}
```
And in the plan prose, name the person/role responsible for provisioning the secret and add a one-line note that rotation requires updating both the Rebar Bot identity ticket and this repo secret.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instead of hardcoding `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`, set a new repo variable (e.g. `REBAR_BOT_EMAIL`) or reuse `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` with the bot address as its default fallback (`${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com' }}`), consistent with the existing pattern in `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` line 100 and `reconcile-bridge.yml` lines 196/235.
findings.finding: The plan hardcodes the committer email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` directly in the workflow rather than routing through the existing `${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL }}` variable-indirection already used by both `reconcile-bridge.yml` and the current `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` (line 100–104). This introduces a second config surface for the bot email: operators who customise `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` for their deployment will find the canary ignoring their setting, while the `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` value is repo-specific and breaks the 'clients copy it verbatim' template contract stated in the workflow header.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Set the canary's committer email to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`' / AC line 1 / `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` line 100–104
findings.suggested_fix:
In the 'Configure git identity' step, change the email line to:
`git config --global user.email "${{ vars.REBAR_BOT_EMAIL || 'joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com' }}"`
This preserves operator override-ability, keeps the template copyable, and avoids adding a second hard-coded email surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the committer email change in the canary is implemented as a hardcoded override in reconcile-bridge-canary.yml (leaving reconcile-bridge.yml unaffected) or via updating the shared `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` repo variable (which also affects reconcile-bridge.yml); enumerate reconcile-bridge.yml as a consumer if the latter.
findings.finding: reconcile-bridge.yml also reads vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL (with the same fallback default 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com') and is an unenumerated consumer: if the plan's email change is implemented by updating the repo variable BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL to joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com, reconcile-bridge.yml would also flip to bot identity, which may be intentional but is unacknowledged.
findings.location: .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml lines 196-200, 235-240; plan body 'Set the canary's committer email to joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan body, specify that the email is hardcoded directly in the 'Configure git identity' step of reconcile-bridge-canary.yml (e.g., replacing `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com'` with a literal `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`) so reconcile-bridge.yml is not affected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] If the BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL default or recommended value changes as part of this ticket, update docs/jira-sync-setup.md to reflect the new value (or explicitly note this as out-of-scope and hardcode the email in the canary only).
findings.finding: docs/jira-sync-setup.md documents BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL with the value 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com' and is an unenumerated consumer: if the shared variable default changes as part of this ticket, the documentation becomes stale.
findings.location: docs/jira-sync-setup.md line 96, 119; plan body (no mention of docs update)

findings.finding: AC1 uses the subjective qualifier 'securely' with no concrete observable definition; the key injection mechanism (tmpfile path, file permissions, GHA `mask`/`add-mask` step, and post-job cleanup) is unspecified, leaving the executor to choose an approach.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: '`reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` injects the signing key securely and sets committer email to the bot.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Step: write key via `echo "$REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY" > /tmp/rebar_signing.key && chmod 600 /tmp/rebar_signing.key`; add a `run: echo "::add-mask::$REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY"` step before any use; add a post step to `rm -f /tmp/rebar_signing.key`. AC1 passes when a log scan (`grep -i signing_key <run-log>`) returns no matches.'

findings.finding: `reconcile-bridge.yml` is an unenumerated consumer of `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL`. The canary's 'Configure git identity' step reads `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL`; the sibling `reconcile-bridge.yml` reads the same repo-level variable at lines 196 and 235. If the plan's email flip is implemented by updating the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` repo variable, `reconcile-bridge.yml` is also affected and should be listed as a co-changed or explicitly acknowledged consumer.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Set the committer email to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`'; `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml` lines 196, 235
findings.suggested_fix: Hardcode `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` directly in the canary's 'Configure git identity' step (overriding the variable only for that job) rather than changing the shared `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` variable, to avoid unintended side-effects on `reconcile-bridge.yml`.

findings.finding: The plan says 'set `identity.signing_key` to it' (the injected file) without specifying the configuration mechanism. The codebase provides two distinct paths: (a) `identity.signing_key` in `rebar.toml` (a committed static file — cannot hold a per-run temp path), and (b) the `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` environment variable override. The plan names neither, so the processing protocol for wiring the key into the signing seam is undefined.
findings.location: Plan body — 'set `identity.signing_key` to it'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'set `identity.signing_key` to it' with: 'Export `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` pointing to the injected tempfile (the env-var override documented in `_config_schema.py`); do not modify `rebar.toml`'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
